chore: add workflow to build and upload release assets (#1613)

- [x] Create `.github/workflows/release-assets.yml` to build and upload
pre-compiled binaries on release
- [x] Support `linux/amd64`, `linux/arm64`, and `darwin/arm64` using
native runners
- [x] Install libvips dependencies per platform (`apt-get` on Linux,
`brew` on macOS)
- [x] Build binary with `go build`, compress to `tar.gz`, and upload to
GitHub Release with `gh release upload`
- [x] Explicitly set `GOOS`, `GOARCH`, and `CGO_ENABLED=1` in the build
step to match matrix targets (CodeRabbit feedback)
- [x] Add `--clobber` flag and quote arguments in the upload step for
idempotent reruns (CodeRabbit feedback)

<!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->



<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Build and attach pre-compiled binaries (tar.gz) to GitHub
Releases</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> During the cleanup and removal of GoReleaser in v2.0.2, the automated
process that built and attached pre-compiled binaries to GitHub Releases
was unintentionally dropped.
> However, our official documentation (Installation Guide) still
instructs users to download the `tar.gz` binary from the releases page
(e.g., `manael_1.x.y_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz`). We need to restore this
functionality to ensure users can install Manael without needing Docker
or a Go toolchain.
> 
> ### Proposal
> Add a new job or workflow in GitHub Actions that triggers when a new
release is published (or a new tag is pushed). This job should
cross-compile the Go binary, compress it into a `tar.gz` archive, and
upload it as an asset to the corresponding GitHub Release.
> 
> ### Tasks
> - Create or update a GitHub Actions workflow (e.g., triggered on
`release: published`).
> - Build the binary for at least `linux/amd64` (and ideally
`linux/arm64` and `darwin/arm64` if applicable).
> - Compress the binary into a `tar.gz` format.
> - Upload the `.tar.gz` file to the GitHub Release using the `gh
release upload` CLI or a community action like
`softprops/action-gh-release`.
> 
> ### Example Workflow Snippet
> ```yaml
> name: Upload Release Assets
> 
> on:
>   release:
>     types: [published]
> 
> jobs:
>   build-and-upload:
>     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
>     steps:
>       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
>       - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
>         with:
>           go-version: '1.24'
>       
>       - name: Build and Compress
>         run: |
>           # Example for Linux amd64
> GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -o manael cmd/manael/main.go
> tar -czvf manael_${{ github.event.release.tag_name
}}_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz manael
>       
>       - name: Upload Asset
>         env:
>           GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
>         run: |
> gh release upload ${{ github.event.release.tag_name }} manael_${{
github.event.release.tag_name }}_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz
> ```</issue_description>
>

commit.message:
Fix workspace tree to preserve expanded subtrees and add lazy loading guards

The core bug: mergeTree was discarding previously-loaded subtree
children when a periodic tree refresh returned empty children[] at
the depth limit. Now matches piclaw's mergeTree which preserves
previous children when the server returns no children (depth cutoff).

Also added:
- treeSignature() to skip re-renders when visible structure unchanged
- pendingSubtreeRef dedup guard to prevent duplicate subtree fetches
- rAF batching for tree updates to coalesce rapid changes
- lastSigRef invalidation on refresh/toggle-hidden
- handleTreeClick now sets selectedPath for directories and clears
  preview state (matching piclaw behavior)

These changes enable proper lazy expansion for large workspaces where
the tree is built incrementally via on-demand subtree loading.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Implement partial TLS code sequence relocation support

Add handlers for R_X86_64_GOTTPOFF, R_X86_64_TLSGD, and R_X86_64_TLSLD
relocations to enhance TLS support beyond the basic DTPMOD/DTPOFF model.

Changes:
- Add applyGottpoff() helper for Initial Exec TLS model with PC-relative
  GOT references. Includes fallback to direct TP offset computation when
  symbol resolution fails.
- Add applyTlsgd() stub for General Dynamic TLS model. Returns helpful
  error message with compilation flag suggestions instead of silent failure.
- Add applyTlsld() stub for Local Dynamic TLS model. Similar to TLSGD.
- Extract TLS relocation handling into separate functions to reduce
  applyRelaTable complexity (35 vs 38 cyclomatic complexity).
- Add test library libtls_models.c with different TLS access patterns.

Implementation notes:
- GOTTPOFF has partial support with fallback mechanism
- TLSGD/TLSLD are recognized but not fully implemented due to GOT
  management complexity. Most modern libraries use DTPMOD64/DTPOFF64
  instead, which are fully supported.
- Clear error messages guide users to use -ftls-model=initial-exec or
  -mtls-dialect=gnu2 when encountering unsupported relocations.

Testing:
- All existing tests pass (CGO_ENABLED=0 go test ./...)
- Existing TLS libraries (libtls.so) continue to load successfully
- Complexity increased modestly: 32 → 35 cyclomatic (+9.4%)

<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Implement a maximum file size limit for upstream
responses</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> Image processing, especially decoding and encoding with libraries like
`libvips`, can be extremely memory-intensive.
> Currently, Manael buffers the entire upstream response body into
memory (`bytes.NewBuffer(nil)`) before processing it. If the upstream
server returns an exceptionally large file (e.g., a 1GB malicious or
accidental file), it could cause Manael to crash due to Out-Of-Memory
(OOM).
> 
> ### Proposal
> Implement a configurable maximum file size limit for images fetched
from the upstream server. If the `Content-Length` exceeds this limit, or
if the stream reads more than the limit, the proxy should abort the
transformation and either return the original file or a `413 Payload Too
Large` / `502 Bad Gateway` error.
> 
> ### Example Implementation
> We can use `io.LimitReader` when reading the upstream body to prevent
unbound memory growth.
> 
> ```go
> // Define a max size, e.g., 20 MB
> const maxImageSize = 20 * 1024 * 1024 
> 
> func modifyResponse(res *http.Response, opts *ProxyOptions) error {
> 	// ...
> 	// Prevent reading more than the maximum allowed size into memory
> 	limitedBody := io.LimitReader(origBody, maxImageSize)
> 	b := io.TeeReader(limitedBody, p)
> 	// ...
> }
> ```
> *Note: We should also expose this limit as an environment variable
(e.g., `MANAEL_MAX_IMAGE_SIZE`) so users can tune it based on their
container memory limits.*</issue_description>
>

> <issue_title>Feature: Instrument application with
OpenTelemetry</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> As Manael transitions to v3.0 and is increasingly deployed in
cloud-native environments, observability becomes critical. Operators
need visibility into request volumes, latencies, and error rates.
> Before exposing specific metrics formats (like Prometheus) or
distributed traces, we first need to instrument the core application
using **OpenTelemetry (OTel)**.
> 
> ### Proposal
> Integrate the official OpenTelemetry Go SDK and instrument the main
HTTP proxy handler. This will lay the groundwork for both metrics
collection and future distributed tracing, without committing to a
specific exporter backend just yet.
> 
> ### Tasks
> - Add dependencies for `go.opentelemetry.io/otel` and
`go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/net/http/otelhttp`.
> - Wrap the main reverse proxy handler with `otelhttp.NewHandler` to
automatically collect standard HTTP metrics (like request duration,
response size, and status codes).
> - Set up a global MeterProvider (initially using a no-op or stdout
exporter just for testing/validation).
> 
> ### Example Implementation Idea
> ```go
> package main
> 
> import (
> 	"net/http"
> 	"go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/net/http/otelhttp"
> 	// ...
> )
> 
> func main() {
> 	// ... (Setup global MeterProvider) ...
> 
> 	proxyHandler := manael.NewServeProxy(upstreamURL, options)
> 	
> 	// Instrument the handler with OpenTelemetry
> instrumentedHandler := otelhttp.NewHandler(proxyHandler,
"manael-proxy")
> 	
> 	srv := &http.Server{
> 		Addr:    conf.httpAddr,
> 		Handler: instrumentedHandler,
> 	}
> 	// ...
> }
> ```</issue_description>

commit.message:
feat(fractal): vertical fractal stack + traceability system

Fractal vertical: same classify→decompose→execute applied at every layer.
Each agent now knows WHY it exists via a lineage chain (Vision → Epic → Story → Task).

## Lineage context (foundation)
- PatternRun.lineage: list[str] — ancestry chain propagated to every node
- ExecutionContext.lineage: list[str] — available to tools (code_write injection)
- engine.py _execute_node(): [WHY — Lineage] block injected before [Your task]
- workflows/store.py: lineage built from workflow.name + phase.name + initial_task
- epic_orchestrator.py: lineage from mission.workflow_name + phase + mission.brief

## Traceability store (why_log)
- platform/traceability/store.py: SQLite why_log table
  (session_id, artifact_type, artifact_ref, lineage_json, rationale, created_at)
- log_artifact(), get_why(), get_session_why() functions
- GET /api/traceability?artifact=X — why-chain for any artifact
- GET /api/traceability/{session_id} — full session why-chain grouped by type
- Answers: "Why does this code/test exist?"

## Fractal story pattern (#18)
- platform/patterns/impls/fractal_stories.py
- Classify gate: "Is this deliverable in 1 sprint?" → ATOMIC or COMPOSITE
- Decompose: Vision → Epic → Feature → Story (max_depth=3, max_children=6)
- Lineage propagated at each level (sprint context + DoD)

## Fractal tests pattern (#19)
- platform/patterns/impls/fractal_tests.py
- Classify gate: "Is this a single atomic test case?"
- Decompose: Feature → TestSuite → TestCase → Assertion
- Each leaf generates pytest code with lineage in docstring:
  """Why: Feature[auth] → Suite[JWT] → Case[expired token → 401]"""
- Auto-logs to traceability store per generated test

## Fractal QA pattern (#20)
- platform/patterns/impls/fractal_qa.py
- Classify gate: "Is this an atomic Given/When/Then scenario?"
- Decompose into atomic BDD scenarios (max_children=5)
- PASS/FAIL tracked per scenario, backprop report aggregated
- Auto-logs to traceability store

## Lineage in generated artifacts
- tool_runner.py _execute_tool(): on code_write with lineage,
  prepends "# Why: <chain>" / "// Why: <chain>" to file content
  (Python → #, JS/TS/Java/Go → //)
- Logs artifact to why_log automatically

## Traceability UI
- mission_detail.html: collapsible "Traçabilité" panel in sidebar
- HTMX loads /api/traceability/{session_id} on panel open
- Renders artifacts grouped by type (code/test/story/qa) with lineage chains

commit.message:
test: add expected failure support to node_compat test harness (#32451)

## Summary

Extends the node_compat test harness to support **expected failures** —
tests that are known to fail with a specific exit code and/or output
pattern. This avoids disabling tests entirely when we know they fail in
a predictable way.

### Changes

- **`PlatformExpectation` enum** — Platform fields (`windows`, `darwin`,
`linux`) now accept `false` (skip), `true` (expect pass), or `{
"exitCode": N, "output": "pattern" }` (expected failure)
- **`ExpectedFailure` struct** — Describes expected exit code and/or
output pattern with `[WILDCARD]` support
- **`handle_expected_failure()`** — Reports pass when failure matches
expectations, fails when test unexpectedly passes or fails differently
- **`tests/node_compat/schema.json`** — JSON Schema for `config.jsonc`
enabling editor validation and autocompletion
- **Unit tests** — Comprehensive deserialization and logic tests for all
config patterns

### Config examples

```jsonc
// Skip on a platform
"test.js": { "windows": false, "reason": "not supported" }

// Expected failure on one platform
"test.js": { "windows": { "exitCode": 1, "output": "error [WILDCARD]" } }

// Expected failure on all platforms
"test.js": { "exitCode": 1, "output": "error [WILDCARD]" }

// Mixed: passes on linux/darwin, expected failure on windows
"test.js": {
  "linux": true,
  "darwin": true,
  "windows": { "exitCode": 1, "output": "Failed [WILDCARD]" }
}
```

Remove Windows 7 support code from System.Net.Security (#124555)

## Description

Windows 7 is no longer a supported OS. Remove Win7-specific code paths,
dead branches, and outdated comments from System.Net.Security.

### Product code
- **`SslCertificateTrust.cs`**: Replace `IsWindowsVersionAtLeast(6, 2)`
guard with `IsWindows()` — the version check only excluded Win7, which
is no longer reachable. Remove unused `System.Runtime.Versioning` using.
- **`SslAuthenticationOptions.cs`**: Remove comment about SSL2+TLS1.2
incompatibility on pre-Win10 (code stays, it's still correct).
- **`ExtendedProtectionPolicy.cs`**: Update comment to clarify
ExtendedProtection is supported on all Windows versions supported by
current .NET version.
- **`SslStreamPal.Windows.cs`**: Clarify legacy `SCHANNEL_CRED` comment
— it's for older Windows versions, not specific to Win7.

### Test code
- **`TestConfiguration.cs`**: Remove dead
`!PlatformDetection.IsWindows10OrLater` branch in null encryption
detection.
- **`SslStreamSystemDefaultsTest.cs`**: Simplify `IsWindows &&
WindowsVersion >= 10` to `IsWindows`.
- **`SslStreamCredentialCacheTest.cs`**: Update comment referencing Win8
behavioral change from Win7.

Note: The `SCHANNEL_CRED` / `UseNewCryptoApi` dual-path remains — it
serves Win10 builds before 18836 (pre-TLS 1.3), not Win7.

commit.message:
Fix zstd install rules breaking clr.runtime clean build (#125063)

## Summary

Fix CMake build failure when building `clr.runtime` from a clean build
(instead of `clr.native`).

## Problem

When building `clr.runtime` from clean, the install step fails with:

```
CMake Error at _deps/zstd-build/lib/cmake_install.cmake:53 (file):
    file INSTALL cannot find
    ".../_deps/zstd-build/lib/zstd_static.lib"
```

This happens because zstd's own `install()` rules are included in the
build's install step. When the `runtime` component install runs, it
tries to install `zstd_static.lib` — but that file was never built
because `libzstd_static` is only built as a dependency of
`System.IO.Compression.Native`, which is not part of the `runtime` build
target.

## Fix

Replace `FetchContent` with a direct `add_subdirectory()` call using
`EXCLUDE_FROM_ALL`, which reliably prevents zstd's own `install()` rules
from being included in the component install. The `EXCLUDE_FROM_ALL`
parameter on `add_subdirectory()` has properly suppressed install rules
since CMake 3.14, making it compatible with the project's CMake 3.26
minimum.

The explicit `install()` calls in
`System.IO.Compression.Native/CMakeLists.txt` that install
`libzstd_static` to the correct destinations continue to work as before
since they target the `libzstd_static` target directly (not via zstd's
own install rules).

Fixes #124234

---------

commit.message:
refactor: centralise path utilities into src/path_utils.rs

## Problem

`lexical_normalize` was duplicated verbatim in `src/store.rs` and
`src/commands/decrypt.rs`, making divergence easy to introduce.
`make_repo_relative` was private to `decrypt.rs` but contained the same
logic as the inline stripping in `store::resolve_store_path`.

Additionally, all three uses shared a latent Windows CI bug: on GitHub
Actions the Windows runner's `TEMP` env var uses the 8.3 short name
(`RUNNER~1`) while `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` returns the long name
(`runneradmin`).  Purely lexical normalisation cannot reconcile these
two forms, so `strip_prefix` always failed on Windows, causing every test
that calls `compute_store_path` or `make_repo_relative` to error with
"source file '…' is outside the repository root".

## Changes

### New: `src/path_utils.rs`

Single source of truth for the three path helpers:

- `lexical_normalize` — resolves `.`/`..` without filesystem access (output
  path construction).
- `normalize_for_comparison` — calls `dunce::canonicalize` when the path
  (or its parent) exists on disk; falls back to lexical normalisation.
  Resolves short names and the `\\?\` UNC prefix on Windows.
- `make_repo_relative` — converts an absolute path to repo-relative using
  `normalize_for_comparison` for the strip-prefix check.

`dunce` (v1.0.5) added as an explicit dependency (was already a transitive
dependency).

### `src/store.rs`

- Removed local `lexical_normalize`.
- Switched `compute_store_path` prefix-check to `normalize_for_comparison`
  (fixes the Windows short-name issue).
- `resolve_store_path` now delegates to `make_repo_relative` instead of
  duplicating the inline strip.

### `src/commands/decrypt.rs`

- Removed local `lexical_normalize` and `make_repo_relative`.
- Imports `crate::path_utils::make_repo_relative`.
- Removed the unit tests that tested the now-deleted local copies; the
  equivalent tests live in `path_utils::tests`.

commit.message:
fix: chat windows no longer grow infinitely — bounded layout + flex scroll

Root cause: .layout had min-height:100vh (unbounded) so .content and
.main-area had no height ceiling. flex:1 + overflow-y:auto on message
containers was useless — parent chain had no upper bound.

Fixes:
- main.css: .layout → height:100vh + overflow:hidden (was min-height:100vh)
  .content: add min-height:0 so grid child respects parent constraint
  .main-area already has flex:1 + overflow-y:auto → normal pages still scroll
- ideation.css: .idea-chat → add flex:1 + min-height:0 so it fills bounded
  parent instead of growing. .idea-messages already has overflow-y:auto ✅
- mkt_ideation.css: same fix for .mkt-chat (used by group_ideation too)

All chat pages (Jarvis /cto, agent /chat, conversation, ideation,
group_ideation) now scroll within their message container instead of
stretching the window.
